seo.title: AI Governance Gateway for LLM & MCP Traffic
seo.description: Govern every model call. Control every tool call. Odock.ai gives teams one controlled gateway for LLM and MCP traffic with access policies, security guardrails, budgets, quotas, routing, compliance controls, and audit-ready usage records.

hero.subheadline: One controlled plane for all AI and MCP traffic. Enforce access policies, security, budgets, quotas, compliance controls, custom workflows, and routing before requests reach providers or tools.

pain.title: AI adoption scales faster than AI control.
pain.description: Direct provider access creates unmanaged spend, scattered keys, inconsistent model access, weak auditability, and new agent/tool security risks.

items.title: Shared credentials
items.description: Provider keys spread across apps, agents, notebooks, and CI jobs. Nobody has a reliable access boundary.

items.title: Costs without owners
items.description: Token spend appears after the fact. Teams cannot stop runaway jobs or attribute usage cleanly.

items.title: Agents with broad tool access
items.description: MCP tools can reach sensitive systems, but access rules and audit evidence remain inconsistent.

items.title: Security logic scattered
items.description: Prompt safety, DLP, rate limits, and custom checks are reimplemented differently by every team.

lifecycle.title: One request path. Every control in place.
lifecycle.description: Every LLM or MCP request passes through the same governance lifecycle. Blocked before upstream on access, safety, budget, or compliance failures.

platform.title: Governance your teams can actually operate.
platform.description: Real platform workflows, not abstract dashboards. Configure keys, policies, budgets, and observe everything from one control plane.

tabs.label: Virtual API Keys
tabs.caption: Give each application, team, or user a governed runtime identity.

tabs.label: MCP Governance
tabs.caption: Allow approved tools, block risky actions, and track MCP usage.

tabs.label: Traffic Analytics
tabs.caption: See traffic, latency, token usage, cost, and trace data.

tabs.label: Usage Records
tabs.caption: Investigate requests without storing prompt content.

tabs.label: Policies
tabs.caption: Apply broad defaults and workload-specific constraints.

mcp.title: Models answer. Tools act. Govern both easily.
mcp.description: LLM access is only half the production boundary. When agents can call MCP tools, they can read data, trigger workflows, and reach sensitive systems. Odock governs model traffic and MCP traffic through the same virtual-key, policy, cost, and evidence layer.

llm.title: LLM Traffic
llm.subtitle: Model calls and completions
llm.code:
# Route LLM traffic through Odock
curl -X POST https://api.odock.ai/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer vk_team_frontend_***" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model": "gpt-4", "messages": [...]}'

# Response includes governance metadata
{
  "choices": [...],
  "usage": { "prompt_tokens": 100, "completion_tokens": 50 },
  "meta": {
    "key": "vk_team_frontend",
    "cost": "$0.0045",
    "latency_ms": 847
  }
}

examples.code:
# Call Odock's OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint directly
# The virtual key replaces any upstream provider key
curl -X POST https://api.odock.ai/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer vk_team_frontend_***" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model": "gpt-4", "messages": [...]}'

# Odock adds usage and governance metadata to the response
{
  "choices": [...],
  "usage": { "prompt_tokens": 100, "completion_tokens": 50 },
  "meta": {
    "key": "vk_team_frontend",
    "cost": "$0.0045",
    "latency_ms": 847
  }
}

import os
import httpx

# Point plain HTTP code at Odock instead of a provider SDK
base_url = os.environ.get("ODOCK_BASE_URL", "https://api.odock.ai/v1")
api_key = os.environ["ODOCK_API_KEY"]
model = os.environ.get("ODOCK_MODEL", "gpt-4.1-mini")

response = httpx.post(
    f"{base_url}/chat/completions",
    headers={
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    },
    json={
        "model": model,
        "messages": [
            {"role": "user", "content": "Explain Odock in one sentence."}
        ],
        "temperature": 0.2,
        "max_tokens": 120,
    },
    timeout=60.0,
)
response.raise_for_status()
print(response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])

# Use Odock's unified endpoint through an OpenAI-compatible SDK
import os
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.environ["ODOCK_API_KEY"],
    base_url=os.environ.get("ODOCK_BASE_URL", "https://api.odock.ai/v1"),
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model=os.environ.get("ODOCK_MODEL", "claude-sonnet-4-5"),
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain budget enforcement."}
    ],
    temperature=0.2,
    max_tokens=200,
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)

import os
import httpx

# /v1/llm/chat is Odock's provider-neutral endpoint
base_url = os.environ.get("ODOCK_BASE_URL", "https://api.odock.ai/v1")
api_key = os.environ["ODOCK_API_KEY"]
model = os.environ.get("ODOCK_MODEL", "claude-sonnet-4-5")

response = httpx.post(
    f"{base_url}/llm/chat",
    headers={
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    },
    json={
        "model": model,
        "messages": [
            {"role": "user", "content": "Explain budget enforcement."}
        ],
        "temperature": 0.2,
        "max_tokens": 200,
    },
    timeout=60.0,
)

response.raise_for_status()
data = response.json()
print(data["content"])

# Use Odock's native OpenAI-compatible endpoints directly
import os
from openai import OpenAI

# Native OpenAI-compatible usage keeps the OpenAI SDK surface
client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.environ["ODOCK_API_KEY"],
    base_url=os.environ.get("ODOCK_BASE_URL", "https://api.odock.ai/v1"),
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model=os.environ.get("ODOCK_MODEL", "gpt-4.1-mini"),
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a short status update."}],
    temperature=0.3,
    max_tokens=120,
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Use Odock's native Anthropic-compatible endpoint directly
import os
from anthropic import Anthropic

# Anthropic's client expects the gateway root, not /v1
client = Anthropic(
    api_key=os.environ["ODOCK_API_KEY"],
    base_url=os.environ.get("ODOCK_GATEWAY_URL", "https://api.odock.ai"),
)

message = client.messages.create(
    model=os.environ.get("ODOCK_MODEL", "claude-sonnet-4-5"),
    max_tokens=160,
    temperature=0.3,
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain model access in Odock."}
    ],
)

print(message.content[0].text)

# Use Odock's native Gemini-compatible endpoints directly
import os

from google import genai
from google.genai import types

# Gemini's SDK appends /v1beta, so use the gateway root here
client = genai.Client(
    api_key=os.environ["ODOCK_API_KEY"],
    http_options=types.HttpOptions(
        base_url=os.environ.get("ODOCK_GATEWAY_URL", "https://api.odock.ai"),
        api_version="v1beta",
    ),
)

response = client.models.generate_content(
    model=os.environ.get("ODOCK_MODEL", "gemini-2.5-flash"),
    contents="Hello from Gemini through my gateway",
)

print(response.text)

# Use Odock's MCP endpoint directly from the official Python MCP client
import asyncio
import os

from mcp import ClientSession
from mcp.client.streamable_http import streamable_http_client

async def main():
    async with streamable_http_client(
        os.environ["ODOCK_MCP_URL"],
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['ODOCK_API_KEY']}"},
    ) as (read_stream, write_stream, _):
        async with ClientSession(read_stream, write_stream) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            result = await session.call_tool("get_me", arguments={})
            print(result)

asyncio.run(main())

faq.title: Questions & Answers
faq.description: Before putting AI traffic in production. Clear answers on compatibility, budgets, routing, observability, compliance, and MCP governance.

items.question: What is an AI governance gateway for LLM and MCP traffic?
items.answer: An AI governance gateway sits between your applications and upstream models or tools. It gives you one controlled request path to enforce access policies, budgets, quotas, security checks, routing, and audit-ready usage records before traffic reaches providers or MCP servers.

items.question: Can I use Odock with OpenAI-compatible SDKs and existing apps?
items.answer: Yes. Odock is designed for low-friction migration with OpenAI-compatible traffic and provider-native patterns, so teams can usually switch the base URL, use a virtual API key, and keep the rest of the application flow largely unchanged.

items.question: How does Odock enforce budgets and quotas before model calls?
items.answer: Odock applies budget reservation and quota checks before upstream execution, not only after usage is reported. That lets teams stop over-budget requests, scope limits by team, app, user, or key, and keep AI spend tied to clear ownership.

items.question: Can Odock control MCP servers and AI agent tool access?
items.answer: Yes. Odock governs MCP traffic and LLM traffic through the same control plane. You can approve specific MCP servers, allow or block tools, apply security policies, attribute actions to identities, and keep evidence for agent activity.

items.question: Can Odock support AI compliance and EU AI Act readiness?
items.answer: Odock helps teams implement audit-ready controls around model and tool access, usage records, policy enforcement, budget ownership, and governed workflows. Compliance still depends on your full system design, but the gateway gives security and platform teams a concrete control point for review and evidence.

items.question: What does Odock log for observability and audit readiness?
items.answer: Teams can track requests, latency, token usage, estimated cost, status, model selection, and governed tool activity from one place. This makes troubleshooting, cost attribution, and audit review much easier without spreading provider keys across applications.

items.question: Can Odock route across multiple models and providers without losing control?
items.answer: Yes. Odock can restrict which models a key may call, route requests to approved providers, and apply failover or routing rules based on your configuration. That helps teams change models faster while keeping access boundaries and governance controls consistent.

methods.label: curl
Python
methods.code:
import os
import requests

# Use the governed Odock MCP endpoint
url = f"{os.environ['ODOCK_GATEWAY_URL']}/v1/mcp/github-tools-http"
api_key = os.environ["ODOCK_API_KEY"]

response = requests.post(
    url,
    headers={
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    },
    json={
        "jsonrpc": "2.0",
        "id": "search-1",
        "method": "tools/call",
        "params": {
            "name": "search_repositories",
            "arguments": {"query": "org:odock-ai gateway"},
        },
    },
    timeout=60,
)

# Inspect the governed MCP response
print(response.json())

methods.label: MCP SDK

faq.title: Questions teams ask about MCP gateways
faq.description: These are the recurring questions that come up when teams move from direct MCP server access to a governed gateway layer.

items.question: What does an MCP gateway do that a direct MCP server URL does not?
items.answer: A gateway adds a policy and management layer in front of MCP servers. In practice, that means one controlled entry point for routing, authorization, access policy, telemetry, and lifecycle operations instead of letting every client talk to each server directly.

items.question: Which MCP transports should a gateway support?
items.answer: For remote MCP, Streamable HTTP is the recommended transport, while SSE remains important for backwards compatibility and some existing servers. Teams still use STDIO for local or packaged servers running inside a controlled environment, so a production gateway usually needs to normalize all three.

items.question: How should authentication work for MCP traffic?
items.answer: For machine-to-machine MCP traffic, OAuth client credentials or bearer-token based authentication is the standard pattern. The key point is that the caller authenticates to the gateway, while the gateway can separately validate or inject the upstream authentication required by the target MCP server.

items.question: Can a gateway front remote MCP servers from the official ecosystem?
items.answer: Yes. The official MCP Registry supports remote servers published over Streamable HTTP or SSE, which means a gateway can sit in front of publicly reachable MCP endpoints and standardize how clients connect to them.

items.question: Why put a gateway in front of agent tool calls at all?
items.answer: Because the risk surface is the action, not just the prompt. Once agents can call tools, teams need a place to enforce access rules, payload inspection, spend limits, traffic policy, and audit logging before any upstream system receives a request.

controlPoints.title: Tool calls are a different attack surface from model calls
controlPoints.description: When an agent can write to a database, push to GitHub, or trigger a workflow, the risk boundary is no longer just the prompt. An MCP gateway gives platform and security teams a hard enforcement point on actions, not just outputs.

items.title: Control which tools agents can reach
items.description: Allowlist known MCP servers, expose only approved tools, and block destructive actions before an agent can call them, regardless of what the model decides.

items.title: Enforce policy before execution
items.description: Authenticate every caller, check access grants, inspect payloads, and reject requests that fail safety, budget, or compliance rules before any upstream call is made.

items.title: Generate audit-ready evidence
items.description: Attribute every tool call to a key, app, team, and user. Give security and compliance teams the records they need to review governed agent behavior, or prove it to an auditor.

compliance.title: Close the audit gap in your agent workflows
compliance.description: Compliance programs need answers to specific questions: who was authorized to use which tool, under what policy, with what evidence? An ungoverned MCP layer can't answer those questions. Odock can.
items: Route GitHub, Slack, database, and internal workflow tools through one governed, auditable endpoint
Reduce prompt injection risk by enforcing tool access constraints outside the agent runtime
Produce evidence for internal AI reviews, vendor assessments, and EU AI Act compliance programs
Keep LLM traffic and tool traffic inside the same policy and operational boundary

cta.title: Your agents need a gateway, not just a proxy
cta.description: If your agents can call tools, you need the same level of control you already expect for model traffic. Odock governs both from a single request path.

featureStrip.title: Three teams. One control plane.
featureStrip.description: Odock bridges the teams who build with AI and the teams accountable for what it does.

items.title: Platform teams
items.description: Register every LLM provider and MCP server once. Expose them through one OpenAI-compatible endpoint with virtual API keys, scoped access, and routing policies, with zero changes to agent code.

items.title: Security teams
items.description: Authenticate every caller. Inspect every payload. Enforce prompt injection protection, tool allowlists, and data leakage controls at the gateway, not per-integration.

items.title: FinOps teams
items.description: Set budget limits and token quotas per key, team, or project. Costs are enforced before the upstream call fires, not discovered in the monthly bill.

leftItems.title: Scoped virtual API keys
leftItems.description: Create isolated API keys for every org, team, user, or project. Control model access, MCP permissions, and policy scope without ever exposing provider credentials.

leftItems.title: Enterprise policy enforcement
leftItems.description: Set rate limits, payload caps, concurrency rules, and budget controls across your hierarchy. Odock enforces every policy before the upstream request is made.

leftItems.title: Secure MCP tool governance
leftItems.description: Control which MCP servers agents can access and which tools they can use. Allow trusted actions, block risky ones, and inspect payloads before execution.

leftItems.title: Protected provider credentials
leftItems.description: Keep BEARER, BASIC, and OAUTH2 credentials secured inside Odock. Secrets are injected only at runtime after governance checks pass, never handled by agents.

rightItems.title: Audit-ready AI activity logs
rightItems.description: Track every LLM and MCP request by key, team, user, model, tool, cost, latency, status, and payload size. Built for audits, reviews, and chargeback without custom instrumentation.

rightItems.title: EU AI Act compliance support
rightItems.description: Prepare for high-risk AI obligations with infrastructure-level evidence. Odock shows who accessed each model or tool, under which policy, and with what audit trail.

rightItems.title: AI cost tracking and chargeback
rightItems.description: Attribute LLM and MCP spend by team, project, user, or business unit. Export clean usage records for finance, billing, and operational reviews with no extra pipeline.

rightItems.title: Gateway-level prompt protection
rightItems.description: Stop jailbreaks, data leaks, and unsafe outputs at the AI gateway. Apply consistent guardrails across every team, model, agent, and integration.

deployment.title: Fits your security requirements, not the other way around.
deployment.description: Whether you need the fastest path to production or full data residency, Odock works the way your security team requires.

urgency.description: The Cloud Security Alliance reports 65% of those organizations experienced an AI agent security incident in the same period. By end of 2026, Gartner forecasts 40% of enterprise applications will embed AI agents, up from under 5% in 2025. The gap between AI adoption and AI control is a governance problem. Odock closes it at the infrastructure layer.

integrations.title: One line change. Full governance, immediately.
integrations.description: Odock is OpenAI API-compatible. Change the base URL and API key, and every existing SDK, script, and agent works without modification. OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, Cohere, and any OpenAI-compatible provider available on day one. MCP SDK compatible for tool governance.

code.snippet:
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    api_key="YOUR_ODOCK_VIRTUAL_KEY",
    base_url="https://api.odock.ai/v1",  # ← the only change
)

# Everything else, including models, streaming, and tools, stays identical.

faq.title: What enterprise teams ask before deploying

items.question: How is Odock different from a standard API gateway?
items.answer: Standard gateways handle HTTP traffic generically. Odock is built for AI workloads, with virtual API key hierarchies, model-level permissions, MCP server access grants, token budget reservation before execution, and structured usage attribution across LLM and tool traffic. These are core to the request lifecycle, not add-ons.

items.question: Does Odock support self-hosted deployment for data residency?
items.answer: Yes. Fully self-hosted deployment keeps all traffic inside your infrastructure. Recommended for regulated industries, organizations with data residency obligations, or teams that cannot allow model traffic to egress their environment.

items.question: How does Odock address EU AI Act compliance?
items.answer: The EU AI Act's high-risk requirements, effective August 2, 2026, demand documented controls over who accessed which AI capability, under what authorization, with what outcome. Odock creates that evidence at the infrastructure layer: every request is attributed, every policy decision recorded, every usage record structured for review.

items.question: Can Odock govern MCP tool calls, not just LLM calls?
items.answer: Yes, MCP governance is first-class, not an extension. Odock applies the same authenticate → authorize → inspect → reserve → route → record lifecycle to MCP traffic as it does to LLM traffic. Allowlist servers, restrict tools per key or team, apply semantic filters, and get full usage records on every tool call.

items.question: What does migration actually look like?
items.answer: For LLM traffic: change the base URL and API key. Nothing else. For MCP traffic: point your MCP SDK at the Odock endpoint. Most teams route governed traffic within a single session, with no agent logic refactoring and no new SDKs.

metadata.title: EU AI Act Compliance Gateway, Built in Europe, for Europe | Odock.ai
metadata.description: Odock is a European AI governance platform built to close the infrastructure gap the EU AI Act exposes. Audit-ready records, access policy, and data residency, before August 2, 2026.

hero.title: The EU AI Act doesn't regulate your model. It regulates what you do with it.
hero.description: Most compliance programs focus on data and documentation. The gap is in the request path: who was authorized to use which AI capability, under what policy, with what record. Odock closes that gap at the infrastructure layer, for every LLM call and every MCP tool execution.
hero.urgencyLine: High-risk AI system obligations are enforceable from 2 August 2026. Penalties reach €15M or 3% of global turnover.

euFirst.title: Built in Europe. Designed for European regulation.
euFirst.description: Odock is a European company. We don't treat EU compliance as a checkbox added after the fact for a non-EU product. Data residency, GDPR alignment, EU AI Act readiness, and the ability to deploy entirely within EU infrastructure are design decisions, not add-ons.

pillars.title: European company
pillars.description: Odock is founded and operated in the EU. Our governance design reflects European regulatory expectations, not something retrofitted from a US-first product.

pillars.title: Deploy inside EU infrastructure
pillars.description: Self-hosted and hybrid deployment options mean your AI traffic never has to leave EU soil. Full data residency for organisations with GDPR obligations or sector-specific data localisation requirements.

pillars.title: Regulation-aware by design
pillars.description: Every feature in Odock's governance lifecycle, including access grants, audit records, policy enforcement, and usage attribution, maps to a specific obligation in the EU AI Act. We built it that way on purpose.

deadline.title: 2 August 2026. What it actually means for your AI stack.
deadline.description: The EU AI Act's high-risk system obligations become enforceable on 2 August 2026 under Article 113. This is not a reporting deadline. It is the point at which your AI systems must have documented controls, conformity assessments, and technical evidence in place. The European Commission has proposed a potential extension to December 2027 for some Annex III systems, but regulators and legal advisors are clear: treat August 2026 as binding.

penalties.note: Whichever is higher, and it exceeds GDPR maximums
Applies to most enterprise AI deployers

deadline.penaltyNote: For SMEs and startups, fines are capped at the lower of the fixed amount or the turnover percentage, but even 7% of €2M in annual revenue is €140,000. Material at any stage.

articleMap.eyebrow: How Odock maps to the regulation
articleMap.title: Six EU AI Act articles. One governed gateway.
articleMap.description: The EU AI Act's requirements for high-risk AI systems are spread across six core articles. Odock's governance lifecycle addresses each one at the infrastructure layer, not through documentation alone, but through enforced controls on every request.

items.label: Risk management system
items.requirement: Providers must establish a continuous risk management system identifying, evaluating, and mitigating foreseeable risks throughout the AI system lifecycle.
items.odockResponse: Odock enforces access policy, tool allowlists, payload inspection, and semantic filters on every request, creating a live, operational risk control layer, not a paper process.

items.label: Record-keeping
items.requirement: High-risk AI systems must technically allow for the automatic recording of events (logs) over the system's lifetime, with sufficient traceability to identify risks and reconstruct decisions.
items.odockResponse: Every LLM and MCP request is automatically attributed to key, team, user, model or tool, bytes, latency, status, cost, and policy outcome. Structured and exportable, not application logs.

items.label: Transparency for deployers
items.requirement: High-risk AI systems must be designed so deployers can interpret outputs and understand access paths, including how logs can be collected, stored, and interpreted.
items.odockResponse: Odock's usage records provide exactly the attribution and interpretability layer Article 13 describes, with every request traceable to an identity, policy, and outcome.

items.label: Human oversight
items.requirement: High-risk systems must enable human oversight, including the ability to halt, override, or intervene in system operation.
items.odockResponse: Budget limits, quota enforcement, blocked tool lists, and policy rules give human operators the ability to halt, restrict, or constrain AI and agent behaviour before execution, not after.

items.label: Quality management system
items.requirement: Providers must implement a quality management system covering compliance strategy, development procedures, data management, risk management, and post-market monitoring.
items.odockResponse: Odock's governance lifecycle, authenticate, authorize, inspect, reserve, route, and record, is a technically enforced QMS layer for AI request traffic, not a document-only process.

items.label: Deployer obligations
items.requirement: Deployers of high-risk AI systems must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, assign human oversight responsibilities, and ensure logs are retained.
items.odockResponse: Odock is designed for deployers: virtual API keys create clear accountability, access grants document authorization decisions, and every record is retained and attributable for audit.

complianceChecklist.title: What an ungoverned AI request path can't answer, and Odock can.
complianceChecklist.description: National competent authorities and internal auditors will ask these questions. If your AI infrastructure routes requests directly to providers without a governance layer, you cannot answer them. With Odock, every answer comes from structured records, not manual reconstruction.

items.question: Who was authorized to use this AI capability on this date?
items.withOdock: Virtual API key with named scope, team attribution, and access grant record
items.withoutOdock: Shared provider key, with no individual attribution possible

items.question: What policy was in effect when this request was processed?
items.withOdock: Policy version, budget state, and rule outcomes recorded per request
items.withoutOdock: No policy enforcement at infrastructure layer, and no record of what rules applied

items.question: Which AI tools could this agent access, and which were blocked?
items.withOdock: MCP tool allowlist and blocklist enforced and logged per key and server
items.withoutOdock: Agent had unrestricted access to all tools on connected MCP servers

items.question: Was this request inspected for unsafe or non-compliant content?
items.withOdock: Payload inspection, semantic filters, and guardrail outcomes recorded per request
items.withoutOdock: No inspection layer, so content went directly to provider

items.question: Can you reconstruct the full lifecycle of this AI decision for an auditor?
items.withOdock: Complete record: identity, model or tool, bytes, latency, policy outcome, cost, status
items.withoutOdock: Provider logs only, with no identity, policy, or organisational context

deployment.title: Your AI traffic stays where your data obligations require.
deployment.description: GDPR, sector-specific regulations, and internal data governance policies frequently require that traffic and data remain within EU infrastructure. Odock supports three deployment modes, all with the same full governance feature set.

items.title: EU cloud-hosted
items.description: Odock-managed gateway deployed on EU infrastructure. No servers to operate. All traffic stays within European data centres.

items.title: Self-hosted in your infrastructure
items.description: Deploy Odock entirely inside your own EU environment. All AI traffic stays on your network. No egress to third-party infrastructure of any kind.

items.title: Hybrid
items.description: Control plane managed by Odock. Data plane running inside your EU infrastructure or VPC. Policy management and observability without traffic egress.

deployment.residencyNote: All three modes are compatible with GDPR data processing requirements and EU AI Act technical documentation obligations.

faq.title: What legal, compliance, and platform teams ask

items.question: Does the EU AI Act apply to us if we're deploying third-party AI models, not building them?
items.answer: Yes. Under Article 26, deployers of high-risk AI systems have their own obligations, independent of the provider. These include implementing technical and organisational measures, assigning human oversight responsibilities, retaining logs, and cooperating with competent authorities. If your organisation uses AI in a high-risk context, including recruitment, credit, access to essential services, or critical infrastructure, you are in scope as a deployer.

items.question: What does 'high-risk AI system' mean in practice for enterprise teams?
items.answer: Annex III of the EU AI Act lists the categories: AI used in critical infrastructure, education, employment and HR decisions, access to essential private and public services, law enforcement, migration, and administration of justice. If your organisation operates AI in any of these domains, or deploys AI agents that interact with these systems, you are likely managing high-risk AI systems and subject to the full set of obligations by 2 August 2026.

items.question: What specific records does the EU AI Act require us to keep?
items.answer: Article 12 requires automatic logging of events that enable traceability of the system's functioning, identification of risks, and reconstruction of decisions. The logs must capture inputs, outputs, decision points, and must be attributable to identifiable actors. This is not application logging. It requires structured, attributable, exportable records tied to specific identities and policy states. Odock's usage records are built to this standard.

items.question: How does Odock help with the human oversight requirement in Article 14?
items.answer: Article 14 requires that high-risk AI systems allow human operators to halt, override, or intervene in operation. Odock's budget limits, quota rules, blocked tool lists, and policy enforcement give operators exactly that capability, the ability to constrain or stop AI behaviour before execution, at the infrastructure layer, without requiring application-level changes.

items.question: Does Odock replace our legal counsel or compliance programme?
items.answer: No, and we'd be concerned about any infrastructure tool that claimed otherwise. Odock addresses the technical infrastructure layer of EU AI Act compliance: access controls, audit records, policy enforcement, and data residency. You still need legal and compliance teams to handle classification decisions, documentation, conformity assessments, and regulatory engagement. Odock gives them the evidence and the enforced controls they need to do that work.

items.question: Is the August 2026 deadline really binding, or will it be postponed?
items.answer: Treat it as binding. The European Commission proposed a Digital Omnibus package in late 2025 that could defer some Annex III obligations to December 2027, but this extension has not been formally adopted. Legal advisors and national competent authorities consistently recommend planning for August 2026. The compliance programme you need to run takes time regardless of the final date, and starting late costs more than starting now.

cta.title: The infrastructure gap is the hardest part of EU AI Act compliance to close last-minute.
cta.description: Documentation, risk assessments, and conformity procedures can be written in weeks. A governed request path, with real attribution records, enforced access policy, and audit-ready logs, takes infrastructure changes. The earlier that work starts, the less painful it is.
cta.legalNote: Odock provides technical infrastructure for AI governance. This page is not legal advice. For classification decisions, conformity assessments, and regulatory submissions, work with qualified legal counsel.
